questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman has 2 years of irregular cycles, severe acne, and terminal hair on the lip and abdomen; normal BMI/BP. A morning follicular 17-OH progesterone is markedly elevated and rises sharply on ACTH stimulation. What is the pathophysiology?
options: Partial 21-hydroxylase deficiency → low cortisol, high ACTH, precursors shunted to androgens
questions.explain: This is non-classic CAH (partial 21-hydroxylase deficiency): low cortisol removes feedback, ACTH rises, and precursor 17-OHP backs up into the androgen pathway — mimicking PCOS but distinguished by the high 17-OHP.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old transgender woman on transdermal estradiol and oral spironolactone develops dizziness and muscle weakness. Which lab abnormality is she at highest risk for?
questions.explain: Spironolactone is an aldosterone antagonist, so it spares potassium → hyperkalemia (monitor K and creatinine). Polycythemia is a testosterone risk; severe hyperprolactinemia is a cyproterone risk.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman has 4 months of rapidly progressive hirsutism, voice deepening, temporal balding, and clitoromegaly. Total testosterone 14.2 nmol/L and DHEAS 28.5 µmol/L are both markedly elevated. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Rapid virilization is a red flag for an androgen-secreting tumour. The markedly high DHEAS (100% adrenal) localizes it to the adrenal cortex. An ovarian tumour would raise testosterone with normal/mildly elevated DHEAS.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man has 14 months of infertility. Semen analysis: volume 2.0 mL, concentration 4 million/mL, motility 45%; normal virilization and testicular volume. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe IM testosterone to boost sperm
questions.explain: Concentration <5 million/mL = severe oligospermia, which warrants a genetic workup (Klinefelter, Y-microdeletion). Prolonged abstinence harms the sample; testosterone is contraindicated (suppresses spermatogenesis).

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with heart failure and CKD has 2 months of tender bilateral subareolar breast enlargement, no discharge or skin change. Which initial lab panel is most appropriate?
options: Total testosterone, estradiol, prolactin, hCG, LH, TSH, LFTs, creatinine
questions.explain: The standard 8-test gynecomastia workup screens for hypogonadism, hyperthyroidism, liver/renal dysfunction, and hormone-secreting tumours: total testosterone, estradiol, prolactin, hCG, LH, TSH, LFTs, creatinine.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old phenotypic female has primary amenorrhea, normal breasts, scant axillary/pubic hair, an absent uterus on ultrasound, and a 46,XY karyotype (CAIS). Why is there no uterus?
options: Sertoli MIF regressed the Mullerian ducts
questions.explain: In CAIS the testes form and Sertoli cells make MIF, whose receptors are normal, so the Mullerian ducts regress (no uterus). The androgen-receptor defect only blocks masculinization of the external genitalia → female phenotype.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old man with infertility has a history of learning difficulties and pubertal gynecomastia surgery. He is 195 cm, with small firm 3 cc testes and azoospermia. Which lab profile is expected?
options: Low LH, low FSH, low T
High LH, high FSH, low T
Low LH, high FSH, normal T
questions.explain: Klinefelter (47,XXY): dysgenetic tubules → low testosterone/inhibin → loss of feedback → high LH and FSH with low testosterone (primary/hypergonadotropic hypogonadism).

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with disruptive hot flashes, mild diet-controlled hypertension, an intact uterus, and no breast-cancer/VTE history wants HRT. Which regimen is best?
questions.explain: Intact uterus → must add a progestogen (prevent endometrial hyperplasia). Her hypertension favours transdermal estrogen (bypasses hepatic first-pass, less effect on clotting factors).

questions.stem: A 22-year-old transgender woman starting oral estradiol will add a medication to allow the lowest effective estrogen dose. Which agent and monitoring is correct?
questions.explain: An anti-androgen augments feminization and lowers the estrogen dose needed. Spironolactone antagonizes both androgen and aldosterone receptors → monitor for hyperkalemia and creatinine.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old cross-country runner has 8 months of secondary amenorrhea, BMI 17.5, intense training, and a low-calorie diet; pregnancy test negative. Which hormonal pattern fits?
options: High FSH, high LH, low estradiol
Low FSH, low LH, low estradiol
Low FSH, high LH, low estradiol
questions.explain: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (female-athlete triad): low energy availability suppresses GnRH pulsatility → low LH, low FSH, low estradiol (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism).

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man has erectile dysfunction that began abruptly after a stressful new job and a new partner; he has normal morning erections and a normal exam. What is the etiology and the mechanism of first-line oral therapy?
options: Psychogenic; raises cGMP (PDE5 inhibition) → lowers calcium
questions.explain: Abrupt, situational ED with preserved morning erections = psychogenic. PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) block cGMP breakdown; higher cGMP lowers intracellular calcium → smooth-muscle relaxation.

questions.stem: A 46,XY newborn has ambiguous genitalia with bilateral undescended testes, normal epididymides/vasa/seminal vesicles, but predominantly female external genitalia with a blind vaginal pouch and clitoromegaly. Which enzyme deficiency is most likely?
questions.explain: Normal internal male ducts (testosterone present) but feminized external genitalia points to 5α-reductase deficiency — testosterone can't be converted to DHT, which is required for external virilization.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with PCOS wants to reduce chin/abdominal hirsutism but is actively trying to conceive and uses no contraception. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Spironolactone is first-line and safe while conceiving
Eflornithine cream removes existing terminal hair systemically
Combined OCPs are indicated to raise LH
Spironolactone is contraindicated due to teratogenicity
questions.explain: Spironolactone is teratogenic and contraindicated when conception is possible without reliable contraception. Eflornithine is topical and only slows growth; OCPs lower (not raise) LH; metformin is an insulin sensitizer, not a receptor blocker.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old transgender woman is given injectable leuprolide (a GnRH agonist) every 12 weeks. What is its mechanism of testosterone suppression?
options: Continuous dosing desensitizes pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing LH/FSH
Pulsatile dosing acts on the adrenal glands
questions.explain: Given continuously, a GnRH agonist paradoxically down-regulates and desensitizes pituitary GnRH receptors → LH/FSH fall → gonadal testosterone drops. (Pulsatile GnRH would instead stimulate the axis.)

questions.stem: A couple is being assessed for infertility. Which single test best confirms that the woman is ovulating?
questions.explain: A mid-luteal (day-21) progesterone >30 nmol/L confirms a functioning corpus luteum and therefore ovulation. Day-3 FSH assesses ovarian reserve, not ovulation.

questions.stem: A woman with PCOS and anovulatory infertility wants to conceive. What is the first-line agent to induce ovulation?
questions.explain: Letrozole (an aromatase inhibitor) is first-line for ovulation induction in PCOS. Metformin improves insulin sensitivity but isn't the primary ovulation agent; spironolactone is teratogenic.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with low libido and a borderline-low testosterone wants treatment but also wants to father a child soon. Why is testosterone replacement the wrong choice for fertility?
options: It raises SHBG and lowers free testosterone
It suppresses the HPG axis, collapsing intratesticular testosterone and spermatogenesis
questions.explain: Exogenous testosterone suppresses LH/FSH → intratesticular testosterone plummets and spermatogenesis halts. TRT is contraindicated when fertility is desired; use HPG-stimulating approaches instead.

questions.stem: A man with obesity and type 2 diabetes has a low total testosterone but normal free testosterone and no symptoms of hypogonadism. What explains this?
options: Obesity and T2DM lower SHBG, reducing total but preserving free testosterone
Lab error in the free testosterone
questions.explain: Obesity, T2DM, and glucocorticoids lower SHBG, so the total testosterone reads low while free (active) testosterone is normal. Interpret total testosterone in light of SHBG.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a prior DVT and migraine with aura has bothersome vasomotor symptoms and an intact uterus. If HRT is used, which estrogen route is preferred and why?
options: Transdermal, because it bypasses hepatic first-pass and has lower thrombotic impact
No route is acceptable; her uterus is intact
questions.explain: Transdermal estrogen bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism and has less effect on clotting factors, preferred when VTE risk or migraine-with-aura is present (still add a progestogen for the intact uterus). Her VTE history is a relative/absolute caution overall.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman with irregular menses, hirsutism, and acne (BMI 31) is suspected of PCOS, but the physician wants to exclude an adrenal enzyme deficiency first. Best initial screening test?
questions.explain: A morning follicular 17-OH progesterone is the best initial screen for non-classic CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency). If >6 nmol/L, confirm with an ACTH stimulation test.

cards.prompt: What hormone dominates each menstrual phase, and what triggers ovulation?
cards.answer: Follicular = estradiol (FSH-driven); luteal = progesterone (corpus luteum). Peak estradiol gives positive feedback → LH surge → ovulation.

cards.prompt: How is ovulation confirmed?
cards.answer: A mid-luteal (day-21) serum progesterone >30 nmol/L confirms a functioning corpus luteum.

cards.prompt: When must a progestogen accompany estrogen in HRT?
cards.answer: Whenever the uterus is intact — unopposed estrogen causes endometrial hyperplasia/cancer. Estrogen alone only after hysterectomy.

cards.prompt: When is transdermal estrogen preferred over oral?
cards.answer: With migraine-with-aura, hypertension, obesity, or VTE risk — it bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism and has less impact on clotting factors.

cards.prompt: State the Rotterdam criteria for PCOS.
cards.answer: 2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound.

cards.prompt: How do you distinguish NCAH from PCOS?
cards.answer: Screen with a morning follicular 17-OH progesterone (>6 nmol/L suggests NCAH); confirm with ACTH stimulation. NCAH is partial 21-hydroxylase deficiency.

cards.prompt: What does rapid-onset virilization with very high DHEAS suggest?
cards.answer: An androgen-secreting adrenal tumour (DHEAS is 100% adrenal). Very high testosterone with normal DHEAS points to an ovarian source.

cards.prompt: What is the key safety caution with spironolactone for hirsutism?
cards.answer: It is teratogenic — requires reliable contraception and is contraindicated when trying to conceive. It also raises potassium.

cards.prompt: What do Leydig cells, Sertoli cells, and 5α-reductase each contribute to male development?
cards.answer: Leydig → testosterone (Wolffian/internal); Sertoli → MIF (regress Mullerian ducts); 5α-reductase → DHT (external genitalia).

cards.prompt: Describe the Klinefelter (47,XXY) presentation and hormone profile.
cards.answer: Tall, small firm testes, gynecomastia, learning difficulties, azoospermia; primary hypogonadism with high LH/FSH and low testosterone.

cards.prompt: Why is testosterone replacement contraindicated in men seeking fertility?
cards.answer: It suppresses the HPG axis, collapsing intratesticular testosterone and halting spermatogenesis (also causes erythrocytosis, worsens OSA, shrinks testes).

cards.prompt: What semen concentration defines severe oligospermia, and what does it trigger?
cards.answer: <5 million/mL — warrants a genetic workup (karyotype, Y-chromosome microdeletion). Normal concentration is ≥15 million/mL.

cards.prompt: How do PDE5 inhibitors produce an erection?
cards.answer: NO → guanylate cyclase → ↑cGMP → ↓intracellular calcium → smooth-muscle relaxation. PDE5 inhibitors block cGMP breakdown, amplifying this.

cards.prompt: List the 8-test gynecomastia workup.
cards.answer: Total testosterone, estradiol, prolactin, hCG, LH, TSH, LFTs, and creatinine.

cards.prompt: Why is there no uterus in complete androgen insensitivity (46,XY)?
cards.answer: Testes form and Sertoli cells make MIF (normal receptors), which regresses the Mullerian ducts; the androgen-receptor defect only blocks external masculinization.

cards.prompt: What is monitored on masculinizing vs feminizing therapy?
cards.answer: Masculinizing (testosterone): Hb/Hct (cis-male ranges) for erythrocytosis. Feminizing (spironolactone): potassium for hyperkalemia; cyproterone: LFTs and prolactin.

cards.prompt: A day-21 progesterone confirming ovulation is above:
Menopause is diagnosed after amenorrhea for:
Best initial screen to differentiate NCAH from PCOS:
First-line agent to induce ovulation in PCOS:
DHEAS is produced almost entirely by the:
Klinefelter syndrome shows which gonadotropin pattern?
External male genitalia require testosterone conversion to DHT by:
Severe oligospermia is a sperm concentration below:
A continuously dosed GnRH agonist suppresses testosterone by:
Spironolactone in feminizing therapy requires monitoring for:
Select contraindications to systemic estrogen HRT:
Select the Rotterdam criteria components (2 of 3 needed):
Select recognised risks of testosterone replacement:
Select common causes of hirsutism:
Select factors that LOWER SHBG (raising free testosterone):

cards.text: A day-{{21}} progesterone above {{30}} nmol/L confirms ovulation.
cards.explain: Reflects a functioning corpus luteum in the luteal phase.

cards.text: NCAH is screened with a morning follicular {{17-OH progesterone}} level above {{6}} nmol/L.
cards.explain: Confirm an elevated screen with an ACTH stimulation test.

cards.text: Normal semen: volume ≥ {{1.5}} mL, concentration ≥ {{15}} million/mL, total motility ≥ {{40}}%.
Nitric oxide raises {{cGMP}}, which lowers intracellular {{calcium}} → smooth-muscle relaxation; PDE5 inhibitors block cGMP breakdown.
In {{5-alpha-reductase}} deficiency, internal male ducts are normal but external genitalia are feminized because {{DHT}} is not made.

cards.prompt: Order the workup of a hyperandrogenic woman:
items: Diagnose PCOS by Rotterdam if mimics excluded

cards.prompt: Order the male infertility workup:
Order the HRT decision steps:
items: Confirm bothersome symptoms + candidacy (<60 / within 10 yr)
Add a progestogen if the uterus is intact

cards.prompt: Match the amenorrhea cause to its gonadotropins:
Match the agent to its action in hyperandrogenism:
Match the disorder to its mechanism:
pairs: Androgen-receptor defect; MIF still regresses uterus
No DHT → female external, male internal

cards.prompt: Match the GAHT agent to its monitoring concern:
pairs: VTE risk (prefer transdermal if high risk)

cards.prompt: When is vaginal low-dose estrogen preferred?
cards.answer: For isolated genitourinary symptoms (dryness, dyspareunia) — minimal systemic absorption, so it avoids systemic risks.

cards.prompt: How does 11β-hydroxylase deficiency differ from 21-hydroxylase deficiency?
cards.answer: 11β-deficiency builds up deoxycorticosterone (mineralocorticoid effect) → hypertension, whereas 21-hydroxylase deficiency tends to salt-wasting.

cards.prompt: What features point to psychogenic erectile dysfunction?
cards.answer: Sudden onset, situational occurrence, preserved spontaneous morning erections, and a normal physical exam.

cards.prompt: When should estrogen-containing contraception be avoided?
cards.answer: Migraine with aura, smoker ≥35, VTE/thrombophilia, uncontrolled hypertension, active breast cancer, or <3 weeks postpartum — use a progestin-only method or the copper IUD instead.

cards.prompt: The most effective form of emergency contraception is:
The primary contraceptive action of the progestin in a combined pill is to:
The first physical sign of puberty in boys is:
How do gonadotropins separate central from peripheral precocious puberty, and how is central treated?
cards.answer: Central (GnRH-dependent) has high/pubertal LH/FSH; peripheral has suppressed LH/FSH (hormone from gonad/adrenal). Central is treated with a continuous GnRH agonist to halt progression and preserve height.

cards.prompt: What is the most common cause of delayed puberty, and its hallmark?
cards.answer: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty — a benign, often familial 'late bloomer' pattern with a delayed bone age and normal eventual puberty; reassurance is appropriate.

cards.text: Abnormal uterine bleeding is classified by {{PALM-COEIN}}; postmenopausal bleeding is {{endometrial cancer}} until proven otherwise.

cards.prompt: An adolescent with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche should be screened for:
Distinguish primary from secondary dysmenorrhea and give first-line therapy for primary.
cards.answer: Primary = no pathology, prostaglandin-mediated cramping starting in adolescence → NSAIDs ± combined hormonal contraception. Secondary = underlying disease (endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID) → treat the cause.

cards.prompt: Match the contraceptive to a key feature:

guides.title: Week 27 · 1 Menstrual Cycle, Ovulation & Menopause
guides.html:
Cycle & HRT

Menstrual Cycle, Ovulation & Menopause

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The two-phase cycle and how to confirm ovulation, then menopause and the HRT rules — including who must get a progestogen and who gets the patch.

The cycle in two phases

FollicularLuteal
FSH → follicle → estradiol; endometrial proliferation; "type E" wet mucusCorpus luteum → progesterone; decidualization; "type G" dry mucus; ↑ basal body temp

Peak estradiol gives positive feedback → LH surge → ovulation.

  • Confirm ovulation: a day-21 progesterone >30 nmol/L is the single most useful test.
  • Progesterone challenge: 10–14 days of progestogen — a withdrawal bleed means the endometrium was adequately estrogenized; no bleed = chronic hypoestrogenemia.

Menopause

Diagnosis: 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea with no other cause (loss of follicular activity). Symptoms: vasomotor (hot flashes, night sweats), genitourinary (dryness, dyspareunia), accelerated bone loss, sleep/mood change.

Hormone replacement therapy

Intact uterus → estrogen MUST be opposed
Estrogen alone only if there is no uterus (hysterectomy). With a uterus, add a progestogen to prevent unopposed-estrogen endometrial hyperplasia/cancer.
SituationChoice
Symptomatic, <60 or within 10 yr of menopauseSystemic HRT reasonable
Migraine w/ aura, HTN, obesity, VTE riskTransdermal estrogen (bypasses hepatic first-pass)
Isolated genitourinary symptomsVaginal low-dose estrogen (minimal systemic absorption)
  • Contraindications: undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, breast/estrogen-dependent cancer, active CHD, VTE or stroke history, thrombophilia, active liver disease.
  • Non-hormonal options for vasomotor symptoms: SSRIs/SNRIs, gabapentin/pregabalin, CBT, hypnosis.
Exam pattern: intact uterus + HTN → transdermal estrogen + progestogen (patch avoids first-pass; progestogen protects the endometrium).

guides.title: Week 27 · 2 Female Hyperandrogenism & Amenorrhea
guides.html:
PCOS & mimics

Female Hyperandrogenism & Amenorrhea

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Localize amenorrhea by gonadotropins, separate PCOS from NCAH and an androgen-secreting tumour, and match the anti-androgen to the patient.

Amenorrhea by gonadotropins

PatternLocalizes toCauses
Low/normal LH & FSH, low estradiolHypothalamic/pituitary (hypogonadotropic)Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (female-athlete triad), prolactinoma
High LH & FSH, low estradiolOvarian failure (hypergonadotropic)Turner (45,X), premature ovarian insufficiency

PCOS

Insulin resistance → hyperinsulinemia → theca-cell androgen excess; high LH:FSH → follicular arrest & anovulation. Rotterdam = 2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on US.

  • Manage: lifestyle + metformin (insulin sensitivity); letrozole (aromatase inhibitor) is first-line to induce ovulation.

Non-classic CAH (21-hydroxylase)

The PCOS mimic to exclude
Partial 21-hydroxylase deficiency → ↓cortisol → ↑ACTH → precursor backup into androgens. Presents post-puberty like PCOS (irregular menses, acne, hirsutism). Screen with a morning follicular 17-OH progesterone >6 nmol/L; confirm with ACTH stim. Treat with low-dose dexamethasone at bedtime to suppress the morning ACTH drive. (11β-hydroxylase deficiency instead builds up deoxycorticosterone → hypertension.)

Hirsutism vs virilization

  • Hirsutism: terminal hair in androgen areas; Ferriman-Gallwey ≥6–8. Causes: PCOS (most), NCAH, rarely tumour.
  • Virilization (deep voice, clitoromegaly, temporal balding, ↑muscle) — rapid onset is a red flag for an androgen-secreting tumour. Very high DHEAS (100% adrenal) → adrenal source; very high testosterone with normal DHEAS → ovarian source.

Anti-androgen pharmacology

DrugAction / caution
Combined OCP↑SHBG (lowers free T) + progestin suppresses LH
SpironolactoneAndrogen-receptor + aldosterone blocker; teratogen — needs contraception
Eflornithine creamTopical ODC inhibitor; slows growth, doesn't remove existing hair
MetforminInsulin sensitizer (PCOS)
A woman trying to conceive with PCOS hirsutism: spironolactone is contraindicated (teratogen). Rapid virilization + huge DHEAS = adrenal androgen-secreting carcinoma until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 27 · 3 Andrology: Hypogonadism, Klinefelter & TRT
guides.html:
Male axis

Andrology: Hypogonadism, Klinefelter & TRT

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Sexual differentiation, primary vs secondary hypogonadism, the Klinefelter picture, and why testosterone replacement sterilizes.

Sexual differentiation (who makes what)

  • SRY (Y chromosome) → testis.
  • Leydig cells → testosterone → Wolffian ducts (internal male).
  • Sertoli cells → Mullerian-inhibiting factor → regress Mullerian ducts.
  • 5α-reductase → converts testosterone to DHT → external male genitalia.

Hypogonadism by gonadotropins

Primary (testicular)Secondary (central)
High LH & FSH, low testosteroneLow LH & FSH, low testosterone
Klinefelter, orchitis, chemo/radiationPituitary/hypothalamic disease, hyperprolactinemia

Klinefelter (47,XXY)

Tall stature, small firm testes, gynecomastia, learning difficulties, azoospermia. Fibrosed seminiferous tubules → low testosterone/inhibin → loss of feedback → high LH & FSH (primary hypogonadism).

Testosterone (binding & SHBG)

  • Only 0.5–3% of testosterone is free and active.
  • ↓SHBG (raises free T): obesity, T2DM, glucocorticoids. ↑SHBG (lowers free T): aging, hyperthyroidism, estrogens.
TRT sterilizes
Exogenous testosterone suppresses the HPG axis, collapsing intratesticular testosterone and halting spermatogenesis — it is contraindicated in men seeking fertility. Other risks: erythrocytosis, worsened sleep apnea, testicular shrinkage.
Tall, small firm testes, gynecomastia, azoospermia, high LH/FSH = Klinefelter. Never give TRT to "boost" fertility — it does the opposite.

guides.title: Week 27 · 4 Semen Analysis, Erectile Dysfunction & Gynecomastia
guides.html:
Infertility & ED

Semen Analysis, Erectile Dysfunction & Gynecomastia

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The strict semen-analysis numbers (and when to send genetics), the erection pathway and PDE5 inhibitors, and the 8-test gynecomastia workup.

Semen analysis (strict criteria)

ParameterLower limit
Abstinence before test2–7 days
Volume≥ 1.5 mL
Concentration≥ 15 million/mL
Motility (total / progressive)≥ 40% / ≥ 32%
Morphology (strict)≥ 4% normal forms
  • Investigate after 1 year of unprotected intercourse; confirm with 2 samples (day-to-day variability).
  • Oligospermia <15 million/mL; severe oligospermia <5 million/mL → genetic workup (karyotype, Y-microdeletion); azoospermia = 0.

Erectile dysfunction

PENIS: Psychogenic, Endocrine, Neurologic, Insufficiency of blood flow, Substances. Psychogenic = sudden, situational, preserved morning erections, normal exam.

The erection pathway
Nitric oxide → guanylate cyclase → ↑cGMP → ↓intracellular calcium → smooth-muscle relaxation. PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) block cGMP breakdown. If they fail: intracavernosal alprostadil (± phentolamine/papaverine).

Gynecomastia

  • Physiologic (infancy, puberty, old age) vs pathologic (liver disease, hyperthyroidism, drugs, hCG/estrogen-secreting tumours). Idiopathic is most common overall.
  • 8-test workup: total testosterone, estradiol, prolactin, hCG, LH, TSH, LFTs, creatinine.
  • Manage: reassure physiologic; SERM (tamoxifen) for acute tender disease; surgery for chronic/fibrotic.
Concentration <5 million/mL = severe oligospermia → genetics, not testosterone (TRT worsens it). PDE5 inhibitors work by raising cGMP, not by blocking calcium directly.

guides.title: Week 27 · 5 Gender-Affirming Hormone Therapy & Sexual Differentiation
guides.html:
GAHT & DSD

Gender-Affirming Hormone Therapy & Sexual Differentiation

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Feminizing and masculinizing regimens with their monitoring traps, and the classic disorders of sex development.

Feminizing therapy (estrogen + anti-androgen)

AgentEffect / monitoring
EstradiolBreast development, fat redistribution; VTE/stroke risk — transdermal preferred if >45 or VTE risk. Measure mid-dose, target <735 pmol/L
SpironolactoneAndrogen-receptor + aldosterone blocker → monitor for hyperkalemia (and creatinine)
Cyproterone acetateStronger anti-androgen — monitor LFTs and prolactin; mood effects
GnRH agonist (leuprolide)Continuous dosing down-regulates pituitary receptors → suppresses LH/FSH

Masculinizing therapy

Testosterone (IM/SC/gel) → voice deepening, facial/body hair, clitoromegaly (irreversible). Monitor Hb/Hct using cis-male ranges for erythrocytosis; can worsen OSA.

Two GAHT exam traps
Spironolactone → hyperkalemia (not hypokalemia). The GnRH agonist works by continuous exposure desensitizing the pituitary (paradoxical), not by blocking the receptor peripherally. Discuss fertility preservation BEFORE starting cross-sex hormones (potentially irreversible).

Disorders of sex development

ConditionMechanism / picture
Complete androgen insensitivity (CAIS), 46,XYTestes form; Sertoli MIF regresses Mullerian ducts (no uterus); androgen receptor defect → female external phenotype, absent uterus, scant pubic hair
5α-reductase deficiency, 46,XYNormal internal male ducts (testosterone ok) but ambiguous/female external genitalia (no DHT)
Classic 21-hydroxylase CAH, 46,XXVirilized female genitalia + salt-wasting (low aldosterone) at birth
CAIS has a uterus-absent female with a 46,XY karyotype because MIF still works; 5α-reductase deficiency keeps internal male anatomy but feminizes the outside (no DHT).

guides.title: Week 27 · 6 Contraception
guides.html:
Family planning

Contraception

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

How each method works, the estrogen-containing-method red flags, and what to reach for as emergency contraception.

Methods by mechanism

MethodHow it works
Combined (estrogen+progestin) pill/patch/ringProgestin suppresses LH (blocks ovulation); estrogen stabilizes the cycle + suppresses FSH
Progestin-only pillThickens cervical mucus, thins endometrium, variable ovulation suppression
Depot medroxyprogesterone (injection)Suppresses ovulation; amenorrhea common; reversible bone-density loss
LARC: copper IUDSpermicidal/inflammatory; hormone-free
LARC: levonorgestrel IUDLocal progestin → endometrial atrophy, mucus thickening; reduces bleeding

LARC (IUD, implant) are the most effective reversible methods (least user-dependent).

When estrogen-containing methods are unsafe
Avoid combined (estrogen) contraception with migraine with aura, smoker ≥35, history of VTE/thrombophilia, uncontrolled hypertension, active breast cancer, or <3 weeks postpartum. A progestin-only method or the copper IUD is the safe alternative.

Emergency contraception

OptionNotes
Copper IUDMost effective; up to 5 days after; also ongoing contraception
Ulipristal acetateProgesterone-receptor modulator; effective up to 5 days; more effective than levonorgestrel, esp. later/higher BMI
LevonorgestrelOTC; best within 72 h; less effective with delay/higher BMI
Migraine-with-aura or smoker ≥35 → no estrogen (progestin-only or copper IUD). The copper IUD is both the most effective emergency contraceptive and ongoing contraception.

guides.title: Week 27 · 7 Normal & Abnormal Puberty
guides.html:
Development

Normal & Abnormal Puberty

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The order of pubertal events, and how to separate benign constitutional delay from pathology at both ends.

Normal sequence

  • Girls: thelarche (breast) → pubarche → growth spurt → menarche; first sign is breast budding (~8–13 yr).
  • Boys: testicular enlargement (≥4 mL) is the first sign (~9–14 yr) → pubarche → growth spurt (later than girls).
  • Tanner staging tracks breast/genital and pubic-hair development 1–5.

Precocious puberty (girls <8, boys <9)

Central (GnRH-dependent)Peripheral (GnRH-independent)
Early HPG activation; high LH/FSH; idiopathic (esp. girls) or CNS lesionGonadal/adrenal hormone source; low LH/FSH (suppressed); CAH, tumour, McCune-Albright
Work it up
Bone age (advanced), then LH/FSH (basal + GnRH-stimulated). Central precocious puberty is treated with a GnRH agonist (continuous → pituitary desensitization) to halt progression and preserve adult height. Peripheral causes are treated at the source.

Delayed puberty (no breast by 13 / no testicular enlargement by 14)

  • Constitutional delay (most common) — the "late bloomer," often familial, delayed bone age, normal eventual puberty; reassurance.
  • Hypogonadotropic (low LH/FSH): functional (illness, malnutrition, exercise), Kallmann syndrome (+ anosmia), pituitary disease.
  • Hypergonadotropic (high LH/FSH): gonadal failure — Turner (45,X), Klinefelter (47,XXY).
First male pubertal sign = testicular enlargement. Precocious puberty: gonadotropins separate central (high) from peripheral (low). Constitutional delay is benign with delayed bone age.

guides.title: Week 27 · 8 Abnormal Uterine Bleeding & Dysmenorrhea
guides.html:
Bleeding & pain

Abnormal Uterine Bleeding & Dysmenorrhea

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

A structured cause list for abnormal bleeding (PALM-COEIN), plus primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea and PMS/PMDD.

Abnormal uterine bleeding — PALM-COEIN

Structural (PALM)Non-structural (COEIN)
Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma (fibroid), Malignancy/hyperplasiaCoagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not otherwise classified
Don't miss
Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise → transvaginal ultrasound ± endometrial biopsy. In adolescents with heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche, screen for a coagulopathy (e.g. von Willebrand disease). Always exclude pregnancy first in reproductive-age bleeding.

Workup & management

  • β-hCG, CBC/ferritin, TSH; ultrasound for structure; endometrial sampling if at risk (age ≥45, risk factors, or persistent).
  • Ovulatory dysfunction (AUB-O): hormonal therapy — combined OCP or a levonorgestrel IUD; tranexamic acid or NSAIDs for heavy bleeding.

Dysmenorrhea

PrimarySecondary
No pathology; prostaglandin-mediated cramping starting with menses in adolescenceUnderlying disease: endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID
First-line: NSAIDs ± combined hormonal contraceptionTreat the cause; consider laparoscopy for endometriosis

PMS / PMDD

Cyclic physical and mood symptoms in the luteal phase resolving with menses. PMDD is the severe mood-predominant form — first-line is an SSRI (luteal or continuous) ± a combined OCP.

Postmenopausal bleeding → rule out endometrial cancer. Primary dysmenorrhea is prostaglandin-driven → NSAIDs; new or worsening pain with deep dyspareunia suggests secondary causes like endometriosis.

guides.title: Week 27 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Endocrine III — Reproductive

Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Rapid virilization (deep voice, clitoromegaly) + very high DHEAS → androgen-secreting adrenal tumour until proven otherwise
Salt-wasting classic CAH in a virilized newborn → low aldosterone — life-threatening, needs glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid
Spironolactone in a woman who may conceive → teratogen — mandatory contraception / avoid
Testosterone for fertility → WRONG — it suppresses spermatogenesis; never give TRT to a man seeking conception
Estrogen HRT with VTE/stroke history or active estrogen-dependent cancer → contraindicated

Cycle & menopause Cycle

Follicular / luteal estradiol / progesterone
Confirm ovulation day-21 progesterone >30 nmol/L
Menopause dx 12 months amenorrhea
Intact uterus estrogen + progestogen (always)
VTE/HTN/migraine transdermal estrogen
Genitourinary only vaginal low-dose estrogen

Hyperandrogenism Hyperandrogen

PCOS Rotterdam 2 of 3; metformin + letrozole
NCAH screen morning follicular 17-OHP >6 → ACTH stim
NCAH treat bedtime low-dose dexamethasone
Virilization + high DHEAS adrenal tumour
Spironolactone anti-androgen but teratogen
Eflornithine topical, slows growth only

Male hypogonadism Andrology

Primary high LH/FSH, low T (Klinefelter)
Secondary low LH/FSH, low T (central)
Klinefelter 47,XXY; tall, small firm testes, azoospermia
TRT sterilizes (suppresses HPG); erythrocytosis
SHBG ↓ (free T up) obesity, T2DM, glucocorticoids

Infertility / ED / gynecomastia Repro male

Semen normals vol ≥1.5, conc ≥15M, motility ≥40%, morph ≥4%
Severe oligospermia <5M/mL → genetics
Psychogenic ED sudden, situational, AM erections intact
PDE5 inhibitor blocks cGMP breakdown
Gynecomastia workup TT, E2, PRL, hCG, LH, TSH, LFTs, Cr
Tender gynecomastia trial tamoxifen (SERM)

GAHT & DSD GAHT/DSD

Feminizing estradiol + anti-androgen; transdermal if >45/VTE
Spironolactone monitor hyperkalemia
Cyproterone LFTs + prolactin
GnRH agonist continuous → desensitizes pituitary
Masculinizing testosterone; monitor Hct (cis-male)
CAIS 46,XY, no uterus (MIF), female external
5α-reductase def male internal, female external

Contraception Contraception

Combined progestin blocks LH/ovulation + estrogen
No estrogen if migraine w/ aura, smoker ≥35, VTE
Most effective reversible LARC (IUD, implant)
Emergency: copper IUD most effective; up to 5 days
Emergency: ulipristal up to 5 days; > levonorgestrel
Emergency: levonorgestrel OTC; best <72 h

Puberty Puberty

First sign — girls breast budding (thelarche)
First sign — boys testicular enlargement ≥4 mL
Precocious central high LH/FSH → GnRH agonist
Precocious peripheral low LH/FSH (CAH, tumour, McCune-Albright)
Constitutional delay benign, delayed bone age, familial
Kallmann hypogonadotropic + anosmia

AUB & pain AUB

Cause list PALM-COEIN
Postmenopausal bleeding endometrial cancer until proven otherwise
Heavy bleed from menarche screen coagulopathy (vWD)
AUB-O treatment OCP / LNG-IUD; TXA or NSAIDs
Primary dysmenorrhea prostaglandin-driven → NSAIDs
PMDD SSRI ± combined OCP
Week 27 · Endocrine III — Reproductive · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.contra: Estrogen-dependent cancer, VTE/stroke history, active liver disease
facets.monitoring: Prefer transdermal if VTE/HTN/migraine risk; oppose with progestogen if uterus intact

facets.use: Endometrial protection in HRT; progesterone challenge

facets.mechanism: Aromatase inhibitor — lowers estrogen feedback → FSH rise
Biguanide — improves insulin sensitivity, lowers androgen drive
Ornithine decarboxylase inhibitor — slows follicular proliferation
facets.adverse: Local irritation; does not remove existing hair

facets.mechanism: Glucocorticoid — bedtime dose suppresses the morning ACTH surge

facets.use: GAHT / puberty suppression; central precocious puberty
facets.mechanism: GnRH agonist — continuous dosing desensitizes the pituitary → ↓LH/FSH
Progestin — thickens mucus, suppresses ovulation, thins endometrium

facets.adverse: Reversible bone-density loss, weight gain, amenorrhea

facets.mechanism: Selective progesterone receptor modulator — delays ovulation
